speaker: interviewer
text: Getting up to speed. And I'm recording slate. And go ahead, John.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Mr. Isadore Horne on January 12, 1996 in San Diego, California. My name is John Kent. And we're doing this in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrific. Getting up to speed here, John. And you may start anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is your name, and spell it please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name-- do you want to know my name how which they used to call me in Europe?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both, your original and your current name. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The original name when I was born, they used to call me Itzik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be I-T-Z-I-K, Itzik. Gorenstein with a G, G-O-R-N-S-T-E-I-N, Gorenstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date and current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday was January 3, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what city were you born in? Spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born-- it was not a city. It was a very small little village, a village I would say. We only had about 100 families, 100 families maybe was with 500, 600 people with the children. We lived--

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the town first.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the town was, in Jewish, they used to call us Lozisht. It spells like L-O-Z-I-S-H-T, Lozisht in Yiddish. In Polish, they used to call this Ignatowka. It's similar like [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that Polish version, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish version, Ignatowka, would be I-G-N-A-T-O-W-K-A, Ignatowka. It was in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the names of your family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: The names of my family member, my mother, her name was Rochel in Yiddish, Rochel. And in English would be Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Weintrob, W-E-I-N-T-R-O-B, Weintrob.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father, I don't remember my father. Because my father died in 1918. I was only 2 years old. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The irony is I never saw a picture of him, not in my house. Until today, I never knew how he looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could probably look in a mirror.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the true story is in the old country, Europe, before the war, and especially in small places, people never made pictures, never made pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your brothers and sisters in order oldest to youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest brother, his name was Moshe. I didn't know him either. Because he left for America when I maybe just was born. Or maybe I wasn't even born. I can not know these things. He left to America probably the year 1918 or maybe in those times, whatever, or maybe in '17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold that thought. I have to stop, because your microphone-- OK. We're getting back up to speed. John, just tell them that we had to stop for the mic adjustment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We just had to adjust the mic, so it wasn't so sensitive. You were talking about your brother Moshe. He came to America. And you never met him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never met him. And he died in 1933. I never met. He left here a family with children. And in fact, he took out his wife later, a few years later. I remember that I was a little boy when she left, his wife, with two children. But he died. And now, some of the children still live in different cities here in America. We meet them occasionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had one brother and one sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the oldest brother. Then I had another brother, which his name was Peretz. But he died when I was maybe four or five years old, very little. I hardly remember him either. He was single. He died from a sickness. After that, I had another brother, which he lived already. He was married. He had four children. And he lived at our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Altzei, A-L-T-Z-E-I, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sister, her name was Ritzi. She was older than I am. And she got married just before the war, about four, five years before the war. And she lived in Luck. That was the nearest biggest city to us, approximately, I would say about 35, 40 kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: L-U-C-K?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-C-K or L-U-T-Z-K, Luck. It's very hard sometimes to translate exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Translate, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The way the name the city in Polish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Into English.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, how big was your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: The community-- so this was my family, Altzei with his wife and children, and Ritzi with her children. At the end, at the time before they killed all the people, we lived all together in that little town. What did you ask?

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the community? And what percentage Jewish and Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only Jewish, 100% percent Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how many families did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was 100 families.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Small town.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one long street, one big street. If you stay on this side, it was so straight, you could see the end of the other side and the other end of the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do? Your parents, what was their work like?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Like I said, I didn't have a father. But he was in the religious profession. He was a cantor. He was a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know, whatever that means. My mother and the whole family lived from the farm that we had. And this is what everybody in that little town lived. Everybody had his own farm, his own land, his own car, or maybe sometime two, his own chickens, everything. In other words, we really had everything that we need to eat. We had it from our garden, potatoes, cucumbers, apples, fruit. Everything that really you need, you could get along. And We used to sell the milk. And we had a few Polish zlotys. With that money, we buy bread. Or we needs sugar, which we didn't grow on the field. That's maybe the only thing I can tell you. I've never seen in my life a place so poor like our little village. I thought always that's the poorest place in the whole world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially, without a father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially, without a father. We thought-- when Hitler came to power, we thought Hitler would never come to our place. Because first of all, we are so poor. What is he going to do over there? And then the we couldn't even make a ghetto. Because a ghetto means if there is non-Jewish people, they take them out and separate them. But we were all just Jewish. So there was no use to make a ghetto either. But I guess he find us later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, could you just describe the normal life in that little town before Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the normal life for everybody was just walking in the fields during the day. You come home. You didn't have no electricity. We used to use, most of the time, the moon. Because two weeks, the moon shines. And people used to go early to bed and get up early. And we used to work all day. And then in the winter, of course, we just-- practically nothing. We used to go three times a day to the synagogue. And people used to spend over there a lot of hours. And that's the way life goes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I became 13 years old. When I became 13 years old, I figured this is no place for me to be there. Well, where could I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so poor, I hardly had money to buy a train ticket to go to the place where I wanted to. To go to a college or to a gymnasium was out of the question. Because we didn't have no money to pay for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I find out that the only place for me to go and to learn, to get educated, would be a yeshiva. It's a religious institution that gives out rabbis. When you finish yeshiva, after so many, many years, you can become a rabbi. You learn the Talmud. So I decided me and a few other boy friends. And that was when I was 13 years old-- I mean, 14 years old. So that was in 1929, 1930, in those years. So the yeshiva was good for room and board, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the yeshiva didn't cost anything. The food was for free. And the board was for free. So we decided, a few boys, we went to the yeshiva. It was a long-- I think it was all day with the train. I would say maybe about 400 or 500 miles. But in Poland, 400, 500 miles is not like here in America. 400, 500 miles is nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we finally came to the destination. And the name of the city was Lida, L-I-D-A. It was not too far from Vilna. Vilna was a big city in that area. Vilna. So I learned over there two years. And then I went to higher yeshiva. And that was Baranovich. You want to know how to spell? I think it's written down already. We have that.

speaker: interviewee
text: As in Bara-- Baranovich. And over there I learned another three years. And after five years, I finally came home. I could not come home for any holidays during the five years, because I didn't have the money to buy a train ticket.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother was raising, what, two kids alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother was with two kids. Of course, one was already married. Altzei, the brother. My sister was not married. My sister got married only later when I came already back. In 1935, I was already home, 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 19 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I start working with [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That means I'm preparing for boys who want to go to Israel. Actually, we used to call this a kibbutz, also the name, a kibbutz. And we used to learn trades. We used to do some more to be prepared to go to Israel. Israel was not a state yet. But you need a special permit at that time to go to Israel. But if you belonged to a certain Zionist organization, every Zionist organization through the years got certificates. It was a certificate in order-- you needed the certificate from the English government. Because England, at that time, controlled Israel. Israel was not a state yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was my dream to go to Israel. And I was working with this. Then-- I don't know. I don't remember what happened. I decided-- oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was since my sister got married. And she lived at Luck, which was the nearest biggest city, so I decided I'm going to go. And I'm going to try and find something in a bigger city to do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: What could I do? I was not a professional, I mean, engineer or a lawyer or a doctor. But I knew Hebrew well. So I figured I'm going to go and take some private lessons. So I got some private lessons.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I could support myself. So I rent myself a room. I didn't even live with my sister. And I was there, like, almost two years, from '37 till '39. As you know, in '39, in September, the war broke out between Germany and Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder if we could maybe just slow down a little before we get to '39, just spend a little bit more time. You said that your town originally was entirely Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm wondering how much contact did you and your family have with the rest of the Polish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that an issue at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not have any really connection with the Polish people. Because Polish people did not live around us, only Ukrainian people, Ukrainian. And Polish people, very few sometimes. In the next little town, which I will mention this later, the name was Trochenbrod in Jewish, and in Polish, Sofievka. That was the town there was some Polish people lived there. But not too many. Maybe there was 10 people. There was several thousand Jewish people. Those two little places was strictly Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't have really any connection to tell you. For another reason, if there was some anti-Semitism among the villages with Jewish people, with Ukrainian live together, or Jewish people with Polish people together, there's no question. There was always anti-Semitism only for jealousy.

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't see if the Jewish people lived nice or they have a nice home. But our place, everybody was so poor, they couldn't be jealous of us. So we didn't fight with them. They didn't fight with us. And we lived as good neighbors I could say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, could you talk about your mother a little bit? What kind of a person was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very good person. She never was interested to get married. That was not because she was the kind. Usually, the people in Poland before the war-- we're talking about 50, 60 years ago-- if a husband or wife died, they never remarried. They just lived like this. It's the way it was. And she worked very hard in the fields, took care of the cow, to get milk always from the cow, take care of the chickens. She was very religious. Because not only she, everybody was religious. There was not a question who is reformed, who is conservative, who is Orthodox. Everybody was Orthodox. There wasn't the other kind of groups. Everybody was religious. You knew he has to get up in the morning. He has to go to the synagogue. He has to go in the evening in this synagogue. Sabbath, there's no question. Everybody was in the synagogue. You didn't have to wait for the high holidays, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, to go to the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, beyond the cultural expectations, what were your personal convictions about Jewishness if you had any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, when you're young, you really don't think about those things too much. And then we got suddenly hit, you know, with the war. So we didn't think about religion too much. But everybody was born religiously and was raised religiously. And that's the way we lived. Probably, if Hitler wouldn't have come and killed them, probably they would have been still today over there living that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before we move ahead to 1939 then, during the '30s, when it slowly was building, what were you aware of in that community? How were things slowly changing when Hitler came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean aware of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler was starting to make his speeches and he took over Germany, how did that affect you in your world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very much concerned. We used to talk about that. We used to get together, like, in the synagogue. That was the only place, the only club, we used to get together. And we used to talk about what happened, that year Hitler came to power and this. But nobody ever dreamed or nobody ever expressed that Hitler, the time will come that he will kill all the Jews. This would never anybody thought about it. We thought there will be maybe concentration camps, a sort of labor camps. Not camps to kill the people, but camps. People will work for nothing and things like this. But really, we didn't have to go too far either. There was plenty of anti-Semitism in Poland before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you experience personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't experience. Truthfully, I didn't experience. Why? Because I lived only among Jewish people. How could I experience?

speaker: interviewer
text: True. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I know. I used to read the Jewish papers. I always read the Jewish paper, Like, we have here In Jewish language over there, we used to have a lot of Jewish papers used to go out every day in Warsaw, which was the capital of Poland. I remember the names, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means today, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], moment. All those big Jewish papers, Jewish books, we used to read them. We used to know. We didn't have no television. The fact is, I tell you the truth, we didn't even have a radio in our place, in that small place. But we used to read the newspapers. And I knew exactly what goes on, even we didn't experience that. We knew that there was stories in the gymnasiums, in the colleges, where they separate the Jewish people from the non-Jewish people. And there was a lot of things goes on in this field, how the Polish people don't like the Jewish people. And there was fights, fights in the street many times. We used to read about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you are aware of anything on the other side of Jewish people having negative attitudes towards the Poles and the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I don't know if there's a term for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I never knew anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard anything like that. The Jewish people tried always to live nice with their neighbors. I mean, they worked hard. And we didn't have Jewish people walking around just doing nothing. Everybody tried to do something to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, then let's maybe continue to 1939 where I interrupted you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war started, where were you? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started in 1939, right away we were occupied by the Russians. We were occupied by the Russians. Truthfully, we didn't feel too much of a change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before was Pole, the government. And now, it's the Russian government. Why? The way I told you before. The way we lived personally didn't make it too much difference. As long as they didn't bother us, so we lived nicely and quietly.

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally didn't feel like to stay again in that small village. So I decided I'm going to go to Luck again. And I came there to Luck. And I had a friend of mine. He was a cousin sort of. And he was at that time, like you say, very close with the officials from the Russian government. Because he was a communist before the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I didn't know him. So my friend told me, he say, go and ask him. He's in charge of all the restaurants in the city of Luck. He had the job from the Russian government. Maybe he'll give you a job. So I went to him. And I talked to him. Of course, he knew who I am. So he say, yeah. I offer you a job if you want. There's a big, nice restaurant, one of the finest restaurants just for the big shots, the Russians, the party-- from the party people. And you get the job. I got the job. I worked over there very hard. And the fact is while I was working at that job, I could support my family, too. Because I had a lot of connections with food in the restaurant. My brother used to come once in a while. And I used to help him out. And I was with them from 1939 till 1941, with the Russians. That's where they were there, two years, in Luck. And I will still not forget as long as I live the party that our restaurant had to make for officials from Russia. At that time, his name was Khrushchev. Khrushchev was later the leader of the Russian government. But he was at that time the secretary of the party of Ukraine. And his place was Kiev. Probably you have heard Kiev. It's a big city, one of the big cities in Russia. And that Khrushchev came with a bunch of generals to us to have a conference, a meeting, with the Russians. And that conference and that meeting was in my restaurant, where I was-- restaurant, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. They called it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It means, like, German, something like that. The [NON-ENGLISH], which is like here the FBI. To understand what it means, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is like the FBI. They came a day before, investigate me and talked with me, make sure that everything will be all right. Because a big man like Khrushchev is coming. He's going to eat in the restaurant. And there was a big evening, music. There was dancing and everything and speeches. And they drink the toast to one another. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That means, live, stallion, and Hitler. Because they were buddies at the time. They were friend. Of course, he later attacked them. And they didn't even know. So all evening, they spent singing and dancing and kissing each other in the best friend. And after that convention, after that big evening, I don't remember if it took maybe two months, and the war started between Germany and Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about what date was that restaurant meeting?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about two months before the war. I remember more or less. I don't remember the dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember it was about two months before. And the fact is when the war started, they didn't even know. They just ran away the next day. When the bombs started hitting the city, they start running away. They didn't know that there was a war preparing and that he's going to attack. Because if I would've known, because I was so friendly with everybody, I would've run away with them to Russia, too. But I didn't know either.

speaker: interviewer
text: I get up in the morning, One day, it was war started in-- was in June? No, in May. I really forgot the date when-- Attacked Russia, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: When Germany attacked Russia--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. That's what I was asking.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We have to stop right now.
